coredata.dc:title: Biocompatibility nanoprobe of MXene N-Ti3C2 quantum dot/Fe3+ for detection and fluorescence imaging of glutathione in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Wen
Liu, Huaxiao
Liu, Xuan
Liu, Lixiao
Zhao, Wenbo
coredata.dc:description: MXene quantum dots have attracted much attention due to their great optical performance and excellent water solubility. Glutathione (GSH) plays a key role in living cells. In this study, a biocompatibility nanoprobe was prepared for detecting intracellular GSH based on MXene N-Ti3C2 quantum dots (N-Ti3C2 QDs). The N-Ti3C2 QDs act as the fluorescence reporters and the ferric iron (Fe3+) as the quenchers based on nonradiative electron-hole annihilation. When Fe3+ encounters the amino group of N-Ti3C2 QDs, the electrons of N-Ti3C2 QDs in the excited state will transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-Ti3C2 QDs. When the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe acts on the cancer cell MCF-7, the abundant GSH in the cancer cells can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, which will restore the fluorescence of N-Ti3C2 QDs. The N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe displays a high sensitivity for GSH with a detection limit of 0.17 μM in range of 0.5−100 μM. It becomes a promising probe for detecting and showing cellular imaging of GSH in MCF-7 cells. The N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe might provide a new way for imaging-guided precision cancer diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-Ti3C2 quantum dots
Ferric iron (Fe3+)
Glutathione (GSH) detection
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response to Fe3+ Using N-Ti3C2 QDs as fluorescence reporters
Detection of GSH in vitro by N-Ti3C2 quantum dot/Fe3+ nanoprobe
Cell relative viability and imaging of detecting GSH in MCF-7 cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials
National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials , Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials ; Projects of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ probe and detection of glutathione.
TEM image of (a) N-Ti3C2 QDs and XRD patterns of (b) raw Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 MXene after etched with HF acid and N-Ti3C2 QDs.
Fluorescence spectra of the N-Ti3C2 QDs under different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra of the N-Ti3C2 QDs、N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ and N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3++GSH with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-Ti3C2 QDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom: 0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 60, 80, 100, 120, 150, 200, 500, 800 and 1000 μM) with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. (b) Standard curve of fluorescence intensity versus Fe3+concentration.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ in the presence of different concentrations of GSH (0–100 μM). (b) Standard curve of fluorescence intensity F-F0 versus GSH (0.5-100 μM) concentration.

Confocal microscopy images of GSH detection in living MCF-7 cells. (a) Microscopy image of MCF-7 cells pretreated with NMM (500 μM) for 30 min followed by incubation with the N-Ti3C2 QDs and Fe3+. (b) Microscopy image of MCF-7 cells pretreated with N-Ti3C2 QDs and Fe3+.
Comparison work of other researchers have done with our research results.

head.ce:title: Biocompatibility nanoprobe of MXene N-Ti3C2 quantum dot/Fe3+ for detection and fluorescence imaging of glutathione in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MXene quantum dots have attracted much attention due to their great optical performance and excellent water solubility. Glutathione (GSH) plays a key role in living cells. In this study, a biocompatibility nanoprobe was prepared for detecting intracellular GSH based on MXene N-Ti3C2 quantum dots (N-Ti3C2 QDs). The N-Ti3C2 QDs act as the fluorescence reporters and the ferric iron (Fe3+) as the quenchers based on nonradiative electron-hole annihilation. When Fe3+ encounters the amino group of N-Ti3C2 QDs, the electrons of N-Ti3C2 QDs in the excited state will transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-Ti3C2 QDs. When the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe acts on the cancer cell MCF-7, the abundant GSH in the cancer cells can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, which will restore the fluorescence of N-Ti3C2 QDs. The N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe displays a high sensitivity for GSH with a detection limit of 0.17 μM in range of 0.5−100 μM. It becomes a promising probe for detecting and showing cellular imaging of GSH in MCF-7 cells. The N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe might provide a new way for imaging-guided precision cancer diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is an important antioxidant and free radical scavenger in the body which plays a vital role in maintaining cellular activities [1–3]. For example, GSH can help maintain the function of the normal immune system and has antioxidant effects and detoxification effects [2]. The abnormal amount of GSH in cells is associated with many diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular, Alzheimer's disease, aging, heart disease and other diseases [4,5]. In particular, the content of GSH in cancer cells is significantly higher than that in normal cells, so detecting the content of GSH in cells is crucial for assessing the state of cells [6–8].

The methods for detecting GSH mainly include high performance liquid chromatography [9], mass spectrometry [10], surface enhanced Raman scattering [11,12], electrochemistry [13], and fluorescence spectroscopy. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy is the most common method. Because of its high sensitivity, simple operation, strong stability, good reproducibility, and imaging [14,15]. Recently, it is widely used to detect intracellular substances. At present, most of the fluorescent probes for detecting GSH are organic molecules such as bodipy disulfonate probe [16,17]. However, these fluorescent probes often suffer from the problems of poor photostability, difficult synthesis, high cytotoxicity, poor solubility or irreversible reactivity [18]. Finding the ideal fluorescent probe materials is the direction and main task of the researchers.

Owing to the ultrathin structure and unique physiochemical properties, two-dimensional (2D) materials including graphene and graphene oxide, transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers, and black phosphorus have attracted great attention in the field of biosensing. Among these two-dimensional (2D) materials, MXene is a new type of two-dimensional transition metal carbide or nitride with a chemical formula of Mn+1Xn. Therein, M is a transition metal, including Ti, Zr, V, Nb, Hf, Sc or Mo; X is C or N. MXene is generally produced by selectively etching the A element from Mn+1AXn phases (A is mainly a group IIIA or IVA element), especially Ti3AlC2 [19,20]. Ti3C2 MXene has a good application in photocatalysis [21–24], energy storage and conversion [25–28], lithium batteries [29,30], and electromagnetic adsorption etc [31,32]. At present, quantum dots synthesized by above two-dimensional nanomaterials [33,34] were found that they have owned excellent optical properties, including adjustable photoluminescence, excellent light resistance. They were mainly consist of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [35–39], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [39–43], black phosphorus quantum dots (BP QDs) [34] and molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) etc [44–47]. The QDs obtained from 2D transition metal carbides (MXenes) have attracted more research interest [48,49]. Through the 2D Ti3C2 MXene, QDs derived from MXenes are typically named as Ti3C2 QDs. They have the hydrophilicity, conductivity, and biocompatibility of MXenes, and at the same time exhibit unique optical and photoelectric properties [50,51], and are involved in cell imaging [19] and biosensing [18]. Compared to other probes, N-Ti3C2 QDs have a unique combination of these distinct performance such as easy preparation, functionalization, highly photostabilities and biolabeling potential [19]. In addition to these advantages, the N-Ti3C2 QDs also have excellent biocompatible and chemical inertness in biosensing and imaging. So, there are reasons to believe that the N-Ti3C2 QDs will become a latent ideal fluorescent probe material to realize biological sensing and detection.

In this work, we have synthesized N-Ti3C2 QDs by hydrothermal method and fabricated activatable and biocompatibility N-Ti3C2 QDs nanoprobe with excellent fluorescence performance for intracellular GSH, in which N-Ti3C2 QDs as fluorescence reporters and Fe3+ as quenchers based on nonradiative electron-hole annihilation mechanism (Scheme 1 ). More importantly, the function of intracellular GSH is reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+, making the solution fluorescence intensity in cell solution recovered, which enables the detection of intracellular GSH, thereby farther realizing the evaluation of cell status, which may also provide a method for the diagnosis of cancer cells.

Ti3AlC2 (98 %, 200 meshes) was purchased from Zhongke Leiming Beijing Science and Technology Co., Ltd. HF (≥40.0 %, AR) and L-Cysteine (L-Cys) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine, L-Glutamine (L-Gln), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), Glycine (Gly) and Histidine (His) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. NaCl, ZnCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, CdCl2, Mn(NO3)2, Sr(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, AICl3, D-Aspartic acid (D-Asp), Glutathione(GSH) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide) (MTT) was obtained from J&K Chemical Ltd.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed at 40 kV and 100 mA using Cu Ka radiation with an angle range of 5–90°. The morphology, structure and composition of materials were characterized in detail by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra were obtained by Cary 50 Varian Corporation UV–vis–NIR spectrometer, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by an F-4600 Spectrofluorophotometer from Japan Hitachi Ltd.

Two-dimensional Ti3C2 MXene were synthesized by chemical exfoliation method according to the literature [20]. The N-Ti3C2 QDs were prepared by hydrothermal technology with improvement approach [52]. The concrete synthesis process of N-Ti3C2 QDs is as follows: 0.1 g of the two-dimensional Ti3C2 nanosheets and ultra-pure water (20 mL) were added into a 50 mL of centrifuge tube and the mixture was sonicated for 5 h. Then, 2 mL ethylenediamine and the above mixture were added to a 40 mL teflon-lined, stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was kept at a temperature of 160 ℃ for 12 h in drying oven. Eventually, the N-Ti3C2 QDs were obtained by filtering the mixture with 220 nm membrane. The obtained colloidal solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 12 h (molecular weight cut-off: ∼1 kDa).

200 μg mL−1 N-Ti3C2 QDs and 200 μM Fe3+ were prepared for the further experiments. 800 μL 200 μg mL−1 N-Ti3C2 QDs and 200 μL Fe3+ were as probe substrate to detect different concentrations of GSH (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 μM). The emission spectrum of a mixed solution at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm using a fluorometer was recorded.
Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were cultured in DMEM medium with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% streptomycin/penicillin. Cells were kept in an incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 ℃.

The cytotoxicity were determined by MTT assay with MCF-7 cells. MCF-7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates and every well was added 100 μL of cell suspension in incubator for 12 h. The medium was replaced with different concentrations of N-Ti3C2 QDs and N-Ti3C2 QDs/ Fe3+ for 24 h in incubator. After cultured with the drug, 100 μL MTT solution (including 10 μL MTT and 90 μL DMEM) was added to each well and cultured for another 4 h. Then the MTT solution was aspirated and 100 μL DMSO was added. Finally, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured by using a microplate reader to calculate the relative cell viability.

MCF-7 cells suspension were seeded in the petri dish for 24 h in incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 ℃. Then the N-Ti3C2 QDs solution in DMEM was selected to replace the culture medium in each petri dish. After incubation for 4 h, the cells were washed three times using PBS and incubated with DMEM containing 200 μM Fe3+ for 30 min. Finally, the cells were imaged by a confocal microscope using a 20×objective at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) of the Ti3AlC2, the SEM and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the Ti3C2 MXene were shown in Fig. S1(a), (b) and (c). Ti3C2 MXene powders were obtained by etching Ti3AlC2 with HF acid. Compared to Fig. S1(a), we can clearly see that there is significant delamination after Ti3AlC2 was etched with HF acid in Fig. S1(b). The TEM images of the N-Ti3C2 QDs was shown in Fig. 1 (a), as revealed from the TEM images in Fig. 1(a), the final monodispersing N-Ti3C2 QDs has the average size of 4 nm. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 MXene and N-Ti3C2 QDs were shown in Fig. 1(b). The diffraction of the HF-etched Ti3C2 MXene presented evident peaks at 8.7° and 19°, which agree with the previous XRD results [19,52]. Compared with Ti3AlC2, the most intense peak at 39° belongs to Ti3AlC2 and disappeared in XRD pattern of Ti3C2 MXene, which indicated the completely removal of Al in interlayer after Ti3AlC2 was etched with HF etchant [20,51]. The peak of the N-Ti3C2 QDs observed at 33° and 56°, which agree with the previous XRD results [52]. It illustrated that quantum dots were successfully synthesized.

To further demonstrate whether nitrogen-doped quantum dots N-Ti3C2 QDs were successful synthesized and to verify the structure and the surface composition, N-Ti3C2 QDs and Ti3C2 MXene were investigated using the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. It revealed by XPS data in Fig. S2(a) that the prepared quantum dots mainly contain C, O, N, and Ti, which indicated that the N was doped to the quantum dots. As shown in Fig. S2(b), both of the Ti3C2 MXene and N-Ti3C2 QDs on FTIR spectroscopy showed identical stretching vibrations with OH at 3430 cm−1 and CO at 1630 cm−1 respectively, which reveals that the surfaces of the Ti3C2 MXene and N-Ti3C2 QDs contain OH and CO. Compared with Ti3C2 MXene, the peaks of N-Ti3C2 QDs were appeared at 1068 cm−1, 1378 cm−1 and 1531 cm−1, which was attributed to CO bonds, CN bonds and NH bonds, revealing that the surfaces of the N-Ti3C2 QDs contain CO, CN and NH [19,52].

N-Ti3C2 QDs have strong fluorescence and the emission spectra are generally broad depending on the excitation wavelength, which the emission performance is similar to CQDs and GQDs [35,42]. As shown in Fig. 2 , The emission peaks of N-Ti3C2 QDs gradually shift to the long wavelength side as the excitation wavelength increases continuously from 350 to 500 nm. The strongest peak of N-Ti3C2 QDs appears around 470 nm as excited at 380 nm. Compared with emission peak of Ti3C2 QDs at 450 nm, the strongest emission peak of N-Ti3C2 QDs can get a little red shift [19]. The excellent luminous performance of N- Ti3C2 QDs proves that it is ideal fluorescent probe material and can realize biological sensing and detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response to Fe3+ Using N-Ti3C2 QDs as fluorescence reporters
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that the amino group on the surface of quantum dots can interact with Fe3+ [53–55]. However, the N-Ti3C2 QDs are rich of amino groups. In detail, the electrons of N-Ti3C2 QDs in the excited state will transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-Ti3C2 QDs with the nonradiative electron-hole annihilation [52,56].

We investigated the fluorescence response of Fe3+ to N-Ti3C2 QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence of N-Ti3C2 QDs was quenched when Fe3+ was added to the solution. The fluorescence spectra of the N-Ti3C2 QDs after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ were shown in Fig. 4 . With the concentration of Fe3+ increasing from 5 μM to 1000 μM, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. In the range of the Fe3+ concentration from 5 to 200 μM, there is a good linear relationship between concentration of Fe3+ and (F0-F)/F0 of N-Ti3C2 QDs. The linear equation is (F0- F)/ F0 = 0.0008 [Fe3+] -0.000137 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.996) and the detection limit of Fe3+ was 1.3 μM based on 3σ rule. Besides, as shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity of N-Ti3C2 QDs solution after adding different metal ions (including Fe2+, Na+, K+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+ and Al3+) had no obvious change except for Fe3+. When adding Fe3+ ion to the N-Ti3C2 QDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution decreased to about 18 % of that without Fe3+. Thus, N-Ti3C2 QDs could respond to Fe3+ selectively and sensitively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH in vitro by N-Ti3C2 quantum dot/Fe3+ nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Selective detection is an important indicator for evaluating biosensors. To investigate the performance of N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ sensor for GSH, the fluorescence emission effect of the sensor to interfering substances in cells (including L-Cys, L-Gln, L-Glu, D-Asp, Gly, and His) was studied. Fig. S4 presented the value of intensity ratio F/F0 of fluorescence emission peak intensities of the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ in the absence and presence of interfering substances. The results showed that no any protein biomarker can cause an obvious decrease of F/F0 values besides GSH, this clearly indicating the sensor has a high selectivity to GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell relative viability and imaging of detecting GSH in MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the in vitro compatibility of the N-Ti3C2 QDs and N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+, the MTT assay was carried out. The MTT results in Fig. S5 indicated that the N-Ti3C2 QDs were no significant cytotoxicity at the concentration of 0−200 μg mL−1 of N-Ti3C2 QDs. And the addition of 200 μM Fe3+ to 200 μg mL−1 N-Ti3C2 QDs solution, there was also no significant cytotoxicity to cells. This ensured that the N-Ti3C2 QDs/ Fe3+ can be applied securely to cells as biological materials.

Endowed with rapid response, good biocompatibility and excellent water solubility, the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ probe are appropriate for fluorescence imaging GSH in living cells. The capability of the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ probe monitoring the intracellular GSH levels in living cells was researched. Since GSH can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+ which is rich in cancer cells and make N-Ti3C2 QDs fluorescence restore. It was the first time for N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ detecting GSH in living cells. Firstly, the N-Ti3C2 QDs was incubated with MCF-7 cells for 4 h. In order to confirm whether GSH plays an important role, N-methylmaleimide (NMM, an inhibitor of GSH) was added to the MCF-7 cells in advance. When the Fe3+was added, the MCF-7 cells displayed a little fluorescence in Fig. 6 (a). Conversely, as can be seen in Fig. 6(b), an improved fluorescence intensity was observed in the MCF-7 cells without inhibitor of GSH. It was suggested that N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ probe could detect the intracellular GSH with fluorescence imaging.

N-Ti3C2 QDs with uniform size, excellent optical properties and good biocompatibility were synthesized by hydrothermal method. With the N-Ti3C2 QDs as the fluorescence reporters and Fe3+ as the fluorescence quenchers, a fluorescence nanoprobe (N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+) for detection GSH in living cells was developed. The experimental results show that the N-Ti3C2 QDs/Fe3+ nanoprobe has a wide detection range for the fluorescence detection of GSH, and its detection limit reaches 0.17 μM. This nanoprobe may provide a possibility to realize assessment of the state of cells. It is believed that the probe has promising prospects in clinical diagnosis applications in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National and Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biofunctional Materials; Projects of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.111631.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
